attributes.stem:
You are the intern observing an emergency surgical procedure being undertaken for a ruptured spleen.

The consultant is supervising the case and is berating the registrar for not properly assessing the patient before surgery and not being competent in her operative technique. This goes on for several minutes.

Suddenly, part way through the procedure, there is a large amount of bleeding and the consultant barks at the registrar to get out of the way whilst he takes over. There is a tense silence for the rest of the case.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate action for you as the intern to take?
options: Do nothing apart from showing sympathy to the registrar
Discuss the case with the Head of department
Discuss the case with the anaesthetist present at the case
Raise your concerns directly with the Consultant surgeon
Report the incident to the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency
attributes.explanation:
This is a difficult situation for the intern; it is clear that this is unprofessional behaviour regardless of the competence of the registrar or otherwise. Bullying, intimidation and harassment has no place in medicine or surgery. Equally, this was clearly a charged and stressful operation where the margin for surgical disaster was high. There is a patient on the table with a potentially fatal bleed and emotions can run high. There are often two sides to every story and this could represent a culimination of issues with this registrar. However, despite the junior level of the intern, ignoring what has occurred could lead to a more dangerous situation in the future for any of the personnel involved.

There is no doubt that this was unprofessional but without more knowledge of the facts involved, it is not possible to draw firm conclusions based on this one interaction. Bullying and intimidation from a position of relative power may justify reporting to the head of the unit, human resources, or even AHPRA, but this is not the most appropriate first line method of approaching this particular situation. Is this behaviour typical of the consultant, he is a repeated bully to all staff? Or was the registrar being unsafe and unprofessional in their management and surgery and this had been discussed numerous times before without change in their behaviour and the consultant had had enough and lost personal control of the situation. This would not excuse the behaviour of the consultant but it may explain it. As an intern, it is important to recognise your limitations and times when more senior help is required, be that treatment and management decisions or ethical dilemmas such as this. It is very unlikely that an intern would have the confidence to make a direct approach to the surgeon. The anaesthetist may have had multiple interactions with the surgeons involved and prior knowledge of their behaviour. But they also may not have and the best person to discuss your concerns with in a non-threatening or damaging manner would be the Head of the department. They are in a far better position to be able to have a conversation with the surgeon and raise any concerns or to push this up the line. That is one of their roles.
learningPoints: It is important to recognise your role and place and seek appropriate help from other medical professionals when required.
Bullying of any kind is not tolerated, and must not be ignored.
references: Villafranca A, Hamlin C, Enns S, et al. Disruptive behaviour in the perioperative setting: a contemporary review. Can J Anesth/J Can Anesth 2017; 64:128–40.
